lecture_context.lecture_title: Life After DPO: Post-Training, Reward Models, and Open Alignment Research

unit.name: RewardBench, reward-model evaluation, safety, and DPO reward caveat
unit.description: Introduces RewardBench, explains pairwise reward-model training, summarizes findings, and explains why released DPO models are hard to reuse as reward models.
unit.summary: Lambert asks how alignment work can be evaluated systematically and argues for local evaluation because waiting for Chatbot Arena can take about a month [ts=1491s]. RewardBench is introduced as a transparency tool for reward models, since industry treats reward models as crucial but open researchers lacked ways to measure what makes one good [ts=1558s]. He explains the RLHF feedback loop—policy completions are scored by a reward model—and then describes reward-model training from a prompt, chosen completion, and rejected completion passed through a scalar-output language model; the pairwise loss increases the chosen/rejected score margin [ts=1632s]. He notes quirks such as one-epoch training, low-looking accuracy, ensembles or margin losses, and about 70% annotator agreement, which may reflect real preference diversity rather than pure noise [ts=1710s]. RewardBench manually creates chosen/rejected answers and checks reward-model agreement with human-created preferences, drawing on tasks such as AlpacaEval, MT-Bench, XSTest, and LLMBar [ts=1763s]. Results include fast leaderboard movement, LLM-as-a-judge baselines where Cohere’s specialized reward model beats GPT-4/GPT-4o in this closed domain, a non-saturated Chat Hard subset, and safety/refusal patterns such as over-refusal or answering everything [ts=1888s]. The key DPO caveat is that DPO’s reward is a policy/reference log-probability ratio, not a simple scalar head; without the reference checkpoint, released DPO models cannot reliably be used as reward models and scores plummet [ts=2190s].

key_points.text: RewardBench was built because researchers needed local evaluation tools for reward models.
Reward models train on prompt/chosen/rejected triples and output scalar scores.
The pairwise reward-model loss separates chosen and rejected scalar outputs into a preference margin.
About 70% annotator agreement may be signal because human preferences differ.
LLM-as-a-judge asks a model which answer is better; specialized reward models can outperform general chat models on this evaluation.
Safety evaluation distinguishes correct refusal, over-refusal, and answer-everything behavior.
DPO’s implicit reward depends on policy/reference log-probability ratios, so the reference checkpoint matters.

kp_catalog.name: Reward modeling from pairwise preferences
kp_catalog.description: Turning pairwise human preferences into a reward-model objective based on reward differences. Pairwise reward-model training and RewardBench as local evaluation for reward models.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because reward modeling from pairwise preferences formalizes how turning pairwise human preferences into a reward-model objective based on reward differences, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Safety/refusal evaluation
kp_catalog.description: Benchmarking whether models refuse harmful requests, over-refuse, or answer everything.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because safety/refusal evaluation clarifies benchmarking whether models refuse harmful requests, over-refuse, or answer everything, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: DPO implicit reward/reference caveat
kp_catalog.description: DPO reward depends on policy/reference log-probability ratios, so missing reference checkpoints break reward reuse.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because dPO implicit reward/reference caveat clarifies dPO reward depends on policy/reference log-probability ratios, so missing reference checkpoints break reward reuse, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
